coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence behavior of nanoconjugates of graphene quantum dots and zinc phthalocyanines
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Uddin, Imran
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc phthalocyanines interactions in different modes (covalent and non-covalent) are reported in this study. GQDs were covalently attached to the following complexes: zinc tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc) via amide coupling, zinc tetracarboxyphenoxy Pc (ZnTCPPc) (π–π interaction) and cationic zinc tetrapyridiloxy Pc (ZnTmPyPc) (ionic interaction). GQDs fluorescence was quenched in the presence of the ZnPc derivatives. The nanoensembles of GQDs–ZnPcs showed stimulated emissions of the ZnPcs. The suggested quenching mechanism is through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). These novel nanoensembles hold promise for various optical and luminescence based applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Zinc phthalocyanines
Fluorescence quenching
Förster resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of graphene quantum dots and nanoconjugates 1–3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Covalent linkage of GQDs and ZnTAPc via amide coupling.
Non-covalent interactions of GQDs and ZnTCPPc/ZnTmPyPc via π–π stacking and ionic interaction.
XPS wide scan survey of GQDs (A), high resolution of C1s of GQDs (B), wide scan of GQDs–ZnTAPc (C), deconvoluted N1s spectrum of ZnTAPc (D) and deconvoluted N1s spectrum of GQDs–ZnTAPc (E).
TEM images of GQDs (A), GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) (B), GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) (C) and GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3) (D).
Raman spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) (b), GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) (c) and GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3) (d).
FT-IR spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) (b), GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) (c) and ZnTAPc (d).
XRD of GQD–ZnTAPc (1) (a), GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) (b), ZnTAPc (c) and GQDs (d).
Typical DLS graphs showing average particle sizes of (A) GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3) and (B) GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2).
Ground state electronic absorption spectra of (A) ZnTAPc, (B) ZnTCPPc and (C) ZnTmPyPc. (a) ZnPcs alone (b) linked with GQDs (c) GQDs alone. In 4:1 (v/v) DMF: 50mM PBS.

Emission spectra of; (A) GQDs (a), ZnTCPPc (b), ZnTmPyPc (c) and ZnTAPc (d). Excitation at 340nm for GQDs and 610, 612 and 642nm for ZnTCPPc, ZnTmPyPc and ZnTAPc, respectively, (B) overlay of absorption spectra of all Pcs (b–d, defined as in (A)) with emission spectra of GQDs (a) and (C) Emission (at 340nm excitation wavelength for all) of GQDs alone (a), GQDs mixed with ZnTCPPc (1×10−6 M) (b), (2.5×10−6 M) (c) and ZnTCPPc alone (d) and in 4:1 (v/v) DMF: 50mM PBS solution.
Fluorescence decay curves for GQDs and GQDs–ZnPcs conjugates.

Emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of (A) ZnTAPc (0–2.5μM), (B) ZnTCPPc (0–6.0μM) and (C) ZnTmPyPc (0–5.0μM) in 4:1 (v/v) DMF: 50mM PBS solution. GQDs concentration of 2.0mg/mL. (D) Modified (□) and original (○) Stern–Volmer plots of the interaction of GQDs with ZnTAPc as a representative of other Pcs.
Photophysical parameters for GQDs and GQDs–ZnPcs complexes in 4:1 (v/v) DMF: 50mM PBS. GQDs concentration of 2.0mg/mL.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for Pcs alone.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence quenching and binding constants obtained on addition of ZnPc derivatives to GQDs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence behavior of nanoconjugates of graphene quantum dots and zinc phthalocyanines

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc phthalocyanines interactions in different modes (covalent and non-covalent) are reported in this study. GQDs were covalently attached to the following complexes: zinc tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc) via amide coupling, zinc tetracarboxyphenoxy Pc (ZnTCPPc) (π–π interaction) and cationic zinc tetrapyridiloxy Pc (ZnTmPyPc) (ionic interaction). GQDs fluorescence was quenched in the presence of the ZnPc derivatives. The nanoensembles of GQDs–ZnPcs showed stimulated emissions of the ZnPcs. The suggested quenching mechanism is through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). These novel nanoensembles hold promise for various optical and luminescence based applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials [1]. GQDs are part of the family of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, graphene oxide and carbon dots. The uniqueness of GQDs amongst this class of carbon nanoparticles stems from the presence of quantum confinement and edge effects in their graphene-like cores. Similar to conventional semi-conductor QDs [2], GQDs have a tunable band gap and high photoluminescence activity. Interestingly, GQDs have been found to exhibit superior characteristics compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots and other carbon-based nanoparticles in terms of their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water dispersibility [3], large optical absorptivity, high fluorescence activity and excellent photostability [4]. These properties have thus generated interest in their applications in fields such as nanosensor fabrication, drug delivery, photocatalysis, photovoltaics and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [5,6].

This work reports for the first time on the fluorescence properties of GQDs–Pcs nanoconjugates. The effects of various modes of interactions; covalent and non-covalent on the fluorescence behavior of the host-guest hybrid ensembles are also reported. It is expected that the fluorescence of GQDs will be quenched in the presence Pcs as is the case with semiconductors QDs. Fluorescence quenching of semiconductors QDs by Pcs is well documented [19] as well as FRET as the interaction mechanism between QDs and Pcs [8,19,20].

Natural graphite powder (<20μm), chloroacetic acid, Rhodamine 6G, zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc), dialysis membrane tubing (MWCO 1.5kDa, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS)) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water obtained from a Mili-Q Water system (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. MPcs: Zn tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc, Scheme 1), Zn tetracarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (ZnTCPPc, Scheme 2) and Zn [2,9,16,23-tetrakis[4-(N-methylpyridyloxy)]-phthalocyanine (ZnTmPyPc, Scheme 2) were synthesized according to previously reported procedures [21–23].

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range 300–800nm. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR. A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer, the following parameters were used: the emission was 10mA, the anode (HT) was 15kV and the operating pressure below 5×10−9 Torr. A hybrid lens was used and resolution to acquire scans was at 160eV pass energy in slot mode. The centre used for the scans was at 520eV (width of 1205eV) with steps at 1eV and dwell time at 100ms. as reported before [24]. The high resolution scans were acquired using 80eV pass energy in slot mode. A nonlinear least squares curve fitting procedure was used. The core level binding energies (BEs) were aligned with respect to the C 1s binding energy (BE) of 284.5eV.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (l=1.5405A°, nickel filter). Data were collected in the range from 2θ =10° to 60°, scanning at 1°min−1 with a filter time-constant of 2.5s per step and a slit width of 6.0mm. Details have been previously reported [25].

Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 200, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser (LDH-P-485 with 10MHz repetition rate, 88ps pulse width) as described previously [25].

A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data. Materials characterization for Raman spectroscopy was done using the powdered form of GQDs or their conjugates. Samples were pressed into Raman aluminium sample disc using aluminium funnel set-up with a pestle and placed into the internal sample compartment of the Raman spectrometer for measurement.
Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80kV.

Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out using a Berghof (Germany) High Pressure Laboratory Reactor BR-300, V.3.0 equipped with PT-100 temperature and pressure sensors, BTC-300 Temperature regulator and manometer and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lining.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

The synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) from natural graphite powder has been reported [26]. GQDs were synthesized by the top-down hydrothermal method adopted from the literature with some modifications [27]. Briefly: GO (0.5g) was oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (30mL) for 4h under ultrasonication. The mixture was then diluted with Millipore water (100mL) and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane. The pH of the oxidized GO was adjusted to 8.0 using 10% NaOH. The obtained suspension was transferred to a 400mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 12h. The product was left to cool to room temperature and filtered using a 0.22μm membrane. The collected colloidal solution was further dialyzed for two days using a dialysis membrane (MW 1.5kDa) to remove excess acids and salt. Powdered GQDs were obtained by freeze drying the colloidal solution.

The method employed in this work is an adaptation of the procedure described previously with some slight modifications to achieve covalent linkage of GQDs to ZnTAPc via amide coupling [28]. Briefly: chloroacetic acid (0.2g) and NaOH (0.15g) were added to 20mg GQDs in 10mL Milipore water, followed by ultrasonication for 3h to convert the GQDs surface OH groups into OCH2COO−. After neutralization by 1M HCl, the COOH covered GQDs (carboxylated GQDs) were obtained. This was followed by the incubation of the carboxylated GQDs with EDC (0.025g, 0.13mmol) and NHS (0.013g, 0.11mmol) in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50mM, pH 9.2) at room temperature for 2h under gentle stirring to activate the carboxylic groups. After this time, 20mg (0.26mmol) of ZnTAPc in dimethylformamide (DMF)/PBS solution of (4:1) was added and the mixture further stirred for 24h. The resulting conjugate (GQDs–ZnTAPc) was precipitated out from solution using ethanol and was repeatedly washed with water followed by DMF to ensure that uncomplexed GQDs and ZnTAPc were eliminated.

Adsorption of ZnTCPPc to GQDs surface is through the π–π stacking interaction. This conjugate was prepared following methods previously described for the non-covalent (adsorption) of Pcs to carbon nanomaterials with some modification [10]. Briefly: GQDs (2mg/mL) in 50mM PBS (pH 7.2) were added to ZnTCPPc (0.1g, 0.089mmol) in DMF/PBS (pH 7.2) solution (4:1). The GQDs and ZnTCPPc mixture was ultrasonicated for 4h, followed by stirring for 4 days. The green color of the Pc turned light green due to adsorption to GQDs. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the product. The solid product was dried under vacuum overnight at 60°C. PBS pH 7.2 was employed in this procedure to avoid the deprotonation of the COOH groups of GQDs and ZnTCPPc, hence avoiding any electrostatic repulsion and ensuring that ZnTCPPc is completely adsorbed onto the GQDs via π–π stacking.

GQDs (2mL, 2mg/mL) in 50mM PBS (pH 9.2) were added to 0.2g (0.026mmol) of ZnTmPyPc in DMF/PBS solution (4:1). The GQDs and ZnTmPyPc mixture was ultrasonicated for 4h followed by gentle stirring for 4 days to ensure complete interaction and formation of the nanoconjugates. ZnTmPyPc (Scheme 2) has positively charged quaternary ammonium ions and can electrostatically interact with the negatively charges carboxylate ions of the GQDs under alkaline conditions.

For all the fluorescence quenching measurements, different concentrations (0−6.0×10−5 M) of the respective ZnPcs (ZnTAPc, ZnTCPPc and ZnTmPyPc) were added to a fixed GQDs concentration (2mg/mL) in DMF/PBS solution mixture (4:1). The resulting dispersion was swirled and allowed to stand for 15min for equilibration before measurements. Even though ZnTmPyPc and ZnTCPPc are water soluble, the solvent mixture containing DMF and PBS was employed to ensure solubility of GQDs (which are soluble in water) and to avoid aggregation of the Pcs in water only. Pcs are known to aggregate in water [29,30].

GQDs were excited at 340nm and fluorescence emission spectra recorded between 350 and 600nm. The steady decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was noted and related to the ZnPcs concentrations.

Fluorescence quantum yields (Φ F(QDs)) of the GQDs before conjugation to ZnPcs were determined by the comparative methods as reported before [31]. Using Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
F ( GQDs )
=
Φ F Std
F A
Std
n
2
F Std
A
(
n
S t d
)
2
where A and A Std are the absorbances of the sample and the standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. F and F Std are the areas under the fluorescence curves of the GQDs and the standard respectively and n and n Std are the refractive indices of the solvent used for the sample and standard. Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (Φ F
=0.94 [32]) was used as the standard.

GQDs fluorescence quantum yields after conjugation
(
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
)
with the respective ZnPcs were determined using Eq. (2):
(2)
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
=
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
F
C o n j u g a t e
F
( G Q D s )
where
Φ
F
( G Q D s )
is the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone and was used as the standard, F (GQDs) is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone and
F
C o n j u g a t e
is the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs conjugated to ZnPcs at the excitation of wavelength of 340nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene quantum dots and nanoconjugates 1–3
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized GQDs and GQDs–ZnPcs (ZnPc represents: ZnTAPc, ZnTCPPc and ZnTmPyPc) nanoconjugates were characterized using various techniques as will be shown below. Scheme 1 shows the synthetic route employed for the covalent coupling to form GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) nanoconjugate. GQDs were covalently linked to the amino substituted symmetrical ZnTAPc. GQDs possess COOH and OH groups at the edges of the graphene core, all the OH groups were first converted to COOH groups in the presence of chloroacetic acid and NaOH [28]. The COOH groups were then activated using EDC/NHS, which catalyzed the formation of amide bond between the carboxylic group of the GQDs and the amino groups of the ZnTAPc. Non-covalent interactions of GQDs with ZnTCPPc (to form GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2)) or ZnTmPyPc (to form GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3)) were achieved by π–π stacking and electrostatic (ionic) interactions, respectively, as shown in Scheme 2. π–π stacking is also possible for amide containing GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) depending on orientation and for GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3).

A chemical analysis of the GQDs by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements was undertaken. The results showed the presence of C and O as the prominent elements at 284 and 530eV, respectively (Fig. 1 A). Na auger peak at 497eV is as a result of NaOH employed in the neutralization process of the GQDs precursor. The core level high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum could be decomposed into three chemically distinct components centered at 284.7, 286.3, and 288.2eV, Fig. 1B, which are attributed to sp2 C (CC, CC), sp3 C (CO) and oxidized C (CO), respectively.

Wide scan for GQDs–ZnTAPc conjugate showed the appearance of N1s peak at 398eV (Fig. 1C) which was absent in the spectrum obtained for the GQDs alone (Fig. 1A). Fig. 1D showing core level spectrum of N1s of ZnTAPc alone reveals the following peaks: N-H (400.5eV), NC (399.4eV) and NC (398.3eV). In the deconvoluted N1s spectrum of GQDs–ZnTAPc (1); NC (398.2eV), NC (399.2eV), NH (400.5eV) and NCO (401.5eV) were observed (Fig. 1E). The appearance of the new peak at higher binding energy (401.5eV) is as a result of the successful amide linkage in the nanoconjugates of GQDs–ZnTAPc. This peak occurs at binding energies which have been previously reported for amide linkage in nanoconjugates [24].

TEM analysis was performed in order to determine the size and shape of GQDs nanoparticles. Fig. 2 A shows the representative TEM image of GQDs, showing their monodispersed nature with an overall quasi-spherical morphology and particle size distribution ranging between 2 and 7nm. These nanoparticles appear to be well separated from each other. Fig. 2B–D shows the TEM images of the respective GQDs–ZnPcs nanoconjugates, which showed some aggregation.

The possible origin of the aggregates requires some discussion. Aggregation of Pcs is well documented [30] which has been widely reported to be due to π–π stacking between the rings of the Pcs. The interaction between Pc molecules on adjacent GQDs would result in aggregation. Such self-assembled aggregation of GQDs induced by cytochrome c has been reported [33].

Raman spectroscopic technique was employed to ascertain the quality of the GQDs and their nanoconjugates with the respective ZnPcs. GQDs are known to display characteristic Raman peaks termed the G-band (sp2) tangential mode and the D-disorder band (breathing mode, sp3). As shown in Fig. 3 a, these peaks occurred at 1554cm−1 (G-band) and 1385cm−1 (D-disorder band) for GQDs and the nanoconjugates. The extent of defects or quality of the graphene core of the GQDs can be judged by measuring the ratio of the intensities of D and G bands (sp3:sp2 carbon ratios). The G band is a result of in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms whereas the D band is due to out of plane vibrations attributed to the presence of structural defects. If the D band increases in intensity, it means that the sp2 structure is disrupted and there are more sp3 bonds.

Calculations reveal that the D:G ratio of GQDs is 0.51 which decreases to 0.32 for GQDs–ZnTAPc (1), 0.28 for GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) and 0.21 for GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3). The decrease in the D:G ratio is due to the increase in the intensity of the G band. Increases in the G band have been associated with removal of some amorphous carbons [34]. Since the GQDs are already functionalized with COOH, an increase in the D:G ratio is not expected and not observed. Covalently attached nanoconjugate (GQDs–ZnTAPc (1)) showed the highest D:G value of 0.32 (Fig 3b) compared to the non-covalently attached conjugates, this could be due to differences in the enhancement of the G band.

FTIR spectrum of the linked GQDs–ZnTAPc (1), Fig. 4 b, shows a sharp peak at 3500cm−1 attributable to NH str and there is also a peak at 1659cm−1 (CO, NH amide bend), which further confirms the presence of an amide bond. The characteristic peak at 1659cm−1 was not observed in FTIR spectra of GQDs (Fig. 4a), ZnTAPc alone (Fig. 4d) or for non-covalent nanoconjugates (using GQDs–ZnTCPPc as an example in Fig. 4c). This further confirms the amide coupling between GQDs and ZnTAPc.

Structural characterization of the GQDs–ZnPcs nanoconjugates were carried out using powder XRD and the patterns are shown in Fig. 5 . GQDs are crystalline carbon nanomaterials and their XRD pattern in Fig. 5d exhibit a main (broad) diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.6° which is due to (002) Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers [35]. The broadness of the XRD peak for GQDs reflects their small size. The peak for the Pc alone is observed at ∼2θ =19° (Fig. 5c)and is broad due to the amorphous nature of phthalocyanines [36]. Fig 5a shows the XRD of GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) conjugate with a broad peak at 2θ =22° which is in the range of both GQDs and Pcs. Sharp peaks embedded in the broad peak of the GQDs–ZnTAPc (Fig. 5a), which were not present in Pc alone (Fig. 5c), may reflect increase in size following conjugation as also shown by TEM.

Non-covalent conjugates of GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) shows similar broad diffraction peaks (Fig. 5b), Broader and/or shifted XRD pattern may signify some kind of interaction between the GQDs and Pcs. There was a slight shift from 25.6° for GQDs alone to 23.7° (Fig. 5b) for GQDs–ZnTCPPc, thus indicating the complex formation between GQDs and ZnTCPPc. Similar, observation has been reported for the π–π interactions between GO or single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with Pcs [10,37].

All the as-synthesized GQDs and conjugates were further analyzed for size distribution using dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques. DLS measurements of the GQDs–Pcs conjugates as shown in Fig. 6 A and B (using conjugates 2 and 3 as examples), showed increase in the average particle sizes upon the formation of GQDs–Pcs nanoensembles when compared to GQDs alone at ca 7nm. The size for GQDs alone is in agreement with the TEM images. GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) showed a supramolecular mean diameter size of 21nm, GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3) gave 23nm and GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) showed a mean supramolecular size of 46nm (the latter two shown in Fig. 6).

All nanoconjugates were studied by UV–vis spectroscopy in solutions using a 4:1 mixture of DMF and PBS solution. For the interaction of GQDs with cationic ZnTmPyPc, aqueous solution of 0.1M of NaOH was added to adjust to a constant pH 9.2 so as to keep the carboxyl groups of the GQDs in the ionic state, in order to allow for the electrostatic interaction.

The ground state absorption spectrum recorded for π–π interaction between GQDs and ZnTCPPc is shown in Fig. 7B. As observed for the covalently linked GQDs–ZnTAPc conjugate, a red-shift in the Q-band of ZnTCPPc is also observed from 678nm to 680nm (Table 1) with a sharp increase in absorbance in the blue region (Fig. 7B(b)). Bathochromic shifts in the Q-band may be due to interaction between the π-systems of Pcs [41]. Zhang and Shao have reported the ability of graphene nanosheets or graphene oxide to bind strongly via π–π stacking to ground states (S0) of Pcs [40]. So the binding of GQDs to ZnTCPPc by π–π stacking is easily achievable since GQDs contain planar monolayer sheets of graphene.

Absorption spectra of GQDs, cationic ZnTmPyPc and GQDs–ZnTmPyPc are shown in Fig 7C. Ionic interactions between cationic/anionic Pcs and anionic/cationic fullerenes have been reported [41]. The changes in spectra in Fig. 7C were insignificant for cationic ZnTmPyPc in the presence of GQDs, Table 1. The lack of shift in the Q band could be due to the mainly electrostatic interaction for this conjugate. The B band region shows the presence of both Pc and GQDs.

Fig. 8 A(a) shows the fluorescence emission (445nm) spectra of GQDs (1mg/mL) in mixture of DMF and 50mM PBS solution (4:1) at 340nm excitation wavelength. ZnTCPPc, ZnTmPyPc and ZnTAPc complexes in the absence of GQDs exhibit fluorescence emissions at 687, 694 and 739nm, respectively, as shown in Fig 8A(b–d).

Fluorescence quantum yields (Φ F ) were determined for the GQDs alone and GQDs conjugated with the respective ZnPcs (Table 1). The results as presented in Table 1 show reduction in the
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
of the GQDs–ZnPc conjugates compared to GQDs alone, due to the quenching effect of ZnPcs on GQDs. The covalently linked conjugate GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) has the most reduced
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
. This could be due to the closer proximity of GQDs and ZnTAPc in the fixed amide bonds which guarantees efficient energy transfer process and consequent depopulation of the GQDs excited states.
Fig. 9 shows the fluorescence decay curves for GQDs and the respectively conjugated GQDs; Table 1 shows that there is shortening of GQDs lifetimes on linking to Pcs due to quenching.

For FRET phenomenon to occur there has to be an overlap between the donor (GQDs) emission and acceptor (ZnPc derivatives) absorption. A weak spectral overlap is observed in Fig. 8B, hence some FRET is possible. There was a decrease in the fluorescence intensity (upon excitation at 340nm, where the GQDs absorb and the ZnPc complexes have the B band) of GQDs when titrated with different concentrations of the ZnPc derivatives (Fig. 8C, showing ZnTCPPc as an example). This was followed by an emergence of fluorescence emission around 700nm (Fig. 8C) due to FRET. Since excitation at 340nm will also result in the excitation of the Pc, fluorescence emission was recorded for the respective ZnPc complexes (350–800nm) in the absence of GQDs, at 340nm excitation. There was only a weak emission of the Pcs due to the excitation in the B band area, Fig. 8C(d). It follows from this, that the enhanced emission of the ZnPc complexes in the presence of the GQDs must be due to fluorescence induced (stimulated emission) by the GQDs onto the ZnPc complexes. Similar observations have been reported where there are stimulated emissions in MPcs upon interaction with semiconductor QDs [8,19,20].

FRET efficiency (Eff) was determined experimentally from the fluorescence quantum yields of the donor in the absence (
Φ
F ( Q D )
⁡
) and presence (
Φ
F ( Q D )
l i n k e d
) of the acceptor using equation 3 [42]:
(3)
E f f = 1 −
Φ
F ( Q D )
M i x
Φ
F ( Q D )
The Eff values listed in Table 2 are low due to the weak spectral overlap.

These studies were done by titrating a fixed concentration of GQDs with varying concentrations of the ZnPc derivatives and the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the former was observed, Fig. 10 (Fig. 10 is similar to Fig. 8C, except the latter is extended to the Q band region).

The process of fluorescence quenching is usually divided into dynamic and static quenching. Dynamic quenching would occur as a result of collisional (diffusion-controlled) interactions between the fluorophores and the quenchers and static quenching would originate from the formation of intermolecular complex [42]. Fluorescence data is usually fitted into the well-known Stern–Volmer’s Eq. (4):
(4)
F
0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ
F
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher respectively. kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant of the molecule, K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and
τ F
is the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophores without a quencher and [Q] the quencher concentration.

The Stern–Volmer plots showed positive deviation from the S–V relationship (Fig. 10. inserts) by showing upwards curvature in the plots. The positive deviation (upward curvature) of the plot of F 0/F versus concentration of the phthalocyanines is an indication of the coexistence of both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. The linear S–V plot was obtained by calculating the apparent values of K SV using Eq. (5) (rearranged from Eq. (4)).
(5)
K
S V
=
(
F
0
F
− 1 )
1
[ Z n P c ]

Apparent K SV values were calculated for each concentration of the ZnPcs. The apparent K SV values were then plotted against [ZnPc] (figure not shown) and extrapolated to [ZnPc]=0 to obtain K SV, the Stern–Volmer constant. The K SV values are listed in Table 2, and the largest value was obtained for ZnTmPyPc.

A straight line from F 0/F =1 represents the expected S–V plot, Fig. 10D. The deviation from linearity in the S–V plots (Fig. 10D) was accommodated by treating the data based on a modified Stern–Volmer Eq. (6) that has been derived before to accommodate both dynamic and static quenching mechanisms [42]:
(6)
F
0
F
=
(
1 +
K
S V
[ Z n P c ]
)
( 1 +
K a
[ Z n P c ]
)

The first term in Eq. (6) is the original Stern–Volmer Eq. (4), which represents the dynamic component and the second term is the static component. Ka in the second term is the association constant which was determined by rearranging Eq. (6) and plotting F 0/F(1+ K SV [ZnPc]) versus [ZnPc] using the K SV determined previously. Table 2 shows the value of Ka is largest for GQDs in the presence of cationic ZnTmPyPc (as is the case for K SV) due to electrostatic interactions as will be discussed below.

To further understand the binding interaction between GQDs and Pcs in this study, we also employed the Scatchard Eq. (7) from which the apparent binding constant Kb and binding sites can be found [43]:
(7)
L o g
(
F
0
− F
F −
F
∞
)
= log
K b
+ n log [ Z n P c ]
where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively.
F ∞
denotes fluorescence intensity when the fluorophores are completely saturated with quencher, [Q] is the total concentration of the quencher.

In this study, we synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and studied their fluorescence behaviour in the presence of ZnPcs. To probe the effect of various modes of interaction, GQDs were covalently linked to ZnTAPc via amide coupling. Non-covalent interactions (π–π and ionic) were formed between GQDs and ZnTCPPc and cationic ZnTmPyPc, respectively. The fluorescence emission of GQDs was found to be efficiently quenched by the respective Pcs with a corresponding stimulated emission in the Pcs due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). FRET efficiency was higher for covalent conjugate GQDs–ZnTAPc (1) when compared with the non-covalent conjugates (GQDs–ZnTCPPc (2) and GQDs–ZnTmPyPc (3)). This observation is attributed to the close proximity or stronger binding of the two molecules in the covalently linked nanoconjugate. This study provides fundamental insights into host-guest nanoensembles of GQDs and Pcs which are essential prerequisites to harnessing the synergistic properties of GQDs and Pcs for applications in nanosensors fabrication, photocatalysis, bio-imaging and drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.
